next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

Delroy heard Tiana come in at dawn and kept his eyes shut.

The gate had clicked once, then the soft scrape of somebody lifting it with both hands so the rust would not complain too loud. After that came her careful steps past the window unit, past Micah's sprawled legs, past the place on the floor where the linoleum bubbled up like old skin. Delroy knew the sound of each person in that room. Marcia's steps landed with purpose even when she was tired. Micah's dragged when he thought nobody was watching. Tiana, these days, moved like the house was a test she meant to pass without touching anything.

He lay there under the sheet, hot already, and listened to his daughter set her bag down without a word. He did not ask where she had been. He had no righteous questions left in him. He had used up the right to demand honest answers from anybody in that room.

By seven the apartment had its usual morning smell of bleach, old grease, damp towel, and heat beginning again. Marcia had on one of her house dresses and a scarf tied back from her face, and the sight of her wiping the counter made him feel accused in a way shouting almost never managed. She had a way of cleaning when she was furious that made every object in the room look guilty.

Micah was still under the fan with one foot out from the sheet. Tiana sat at the table with a mug of bush tea and her phone face down beside it. She had changed clothes, but her eyes still looked used, and not from crying. Delroy saw the same shirt from yesterday balled in the laundry bucket. He looked away before his mind started a...
open_hooks_to_carry: Delroy's confession comes from shame, not villain caricature.
Marcia's fury is about being cut out of the household math as much as the missing money.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...hit her face. Sweat slid down her spine under the blouse she had put on for respectability three hours earlier. In the fellowship hall women had looked at her and seen a poor fool. In this kitchen they looked at her and saw, maybe for the first time, how much of the summer she had been trying to hold in one pair of hands.

The rice boiled over. Foam ran down the side of the pot and hissed on the burner. Marcia snatched the dish towel, wrapped the handle, and pulled it half off the flame. Starch slicked across the stove in thick white trails, ugly and immediate. She wiped once, then again, though the mess spread before it cleared.

"Mom," Tiana said, her voice gone soft with something that might have been apology or fear.

Marcia kept wiping.

"Leave it," Delroy said.

"No."

She cleaned until the towel came away gummy, until the burner showed black again, until there was at least one thing in the room that had obeyed her hand. Then she set the towel down and faced them all.

"Listen to me now," she said. "There's no rescue cash. Not in church. Not in Yvette's voicemail. Not in any man saying give him till Monday. What was coming is gone. So if anybody in this room is still waiting on that money to save us, stop waiting."

No one answered. The SCHOOL envelope lay beside Tiana's folder. RENT sat near the sugar bowl. LIGHT had drifted closest to Delroy's elbow, as if even paper knew where accusation liked to settle. The chicken simmered. The fan remained in pieces.

For the first time that summer, Marcia looked at the table and saw nothing there she could divide into a future.
state_deltas_required: Force Delroy to explain the document lie and get a plan strong enough to hold.
Tiana leaves for the night with Malik, and their first sex deepens attachment while exposing how badly she wants a body she can treat as air.
